concept: Google heavily focuses on scalability, distributed systems, and handling massive data. Expect questions about designing systems at Google scale (billions of requests). Key areas: MapReduce, BigTable, Spanner, Borg/Kubernetes, load balancing, and eventual consistency. They also value 'Googliness'—collaboration and handling ambiguity.
common_questions: Design Google Drive
Design YouTube
Design Google Maps
How would you design a distributed cache like Memcached for Google scale?
key_points: Focus on scale: billions of users, petabytes of data
Discuss sharding, replication, and consistency models
Address latency and availability across global data centers
sample_answer: To design Google Drive, I'd use a chunk-based storage system similar to Colossus for file storage, with metadata stored in a distributed database like Spanner. Client apps sync via a changelog service. For conflict resolution, I'd use vector clocks. We'd cache frequently accessed metadata in Redis, and use CDN for sharing public links.

concept: Google looks for 'Googliness'—a mix of curiosity, comfort with ambiguity, and collaborative spirit. They use the STAR method rigorously. Expect questions about handling failure, working with difficult team members, and pushing back on decisions. Show data-driven decision making.
common_questions: Tell me about a time you had to work with ambiguous requirements
Describe a time you disagreed with your manager
How do you handle failure?
Tell me about a time you went above and beyond
key_points: Use STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result)
sample_answer: When our team was asked to build a new service with vague requirements, I set up a 2-day design sprint with stakeholders to define MVP. I created a one-pager with assumptions and got sign-off. We delivered the MVP in 3 weeks, gathered user feedback, and iterated. The final product increased user engagement by 35%.

concept: Amazon interviews are heavily behavioral, based on their 16 Leadership Principles: Customer Obsession, Ownership, Invent and Simplify, Are Right A Lot, Learn and Be Curious, Hire and Develop the Best, Insist on Highest Standards, Think Big, Bias for Action, Frugality, Earn Trust, Dive Deep, Have Backbone, Deliver Results, Strive to be Earth's Best Employer, and Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility. Every answer must map to at least one principle.
common_questions: Tell me about a time you went above and beyond for a customer
Describe a time you had to make a decision without all the data
Give an example of when you pushed back on a decision
Tell me about a time you simplified a process
key_points: Format: 'At [company], I faced [situation]. I did [action] because [LP principle]. The result was [quantified impact]'
Always tie back to customer impact
sample_answer: At my previous company, our API response time degraded by 40% during peak hours. I took ownership and dove deep into the issue, discovering N+1 queries. I invented a caching strategy that simplified the data fetching layer. This reduced response time by 60% and saved $50K/month in infrastructure costs, demonstrating Customer Obsession and Invent & Simplify.

concept: Amazon design questions focus on highly available, fault-tolerant e-commerce and logistics systems. Key concepts: DynamoDB's eventual consistency model, AWS services architecture, handling flash sales (Prime Day), order fulfillment pipelines, and recommendation systems. Expect deep dives into database choices and scaling strategies.
common_questions: Design Amazon's product catalog
Design Amazon's order fulfillment system
Design a flash sale system like Prime Day
Design Amazon's recommendation engine
key_points: High availability and fault tolerance are critical
Use SQS/Kinesis for decoupled, async processing
sample_answer: For a flash sale system, I'd use a distributed cache (ElastiCache) for inventory counts to handle millions of reads. Writes go through SQS to prevent database overload. DynamoDB with on-demand capacity handles order creation. I'd implement a reservation pattern: reserve item in cache, process payment async, confirm or release inventory based on payment result.

concept: Microsoft's culture centers on the Growth Mindset—learning from failures, being open to feedback, and continuously improving. They value collaboration and 'One Microsoft' teamwork. Expect questions about how you've learned from mistakes, adapted to change, and worked across teams.
common_questions: Tell me about a time you learned from a failure
How do you approach learning new technologies?
Describe a time you collaborated across teams
How do you handle feedback?
key_points: Show how you turn failures into growth opportunities
sample_answer: When I failed to anticipate a scaling bottleneck that caused a 3-hour outage, I didn't just fix it—I created a load testing framework and pre-launch checklist that became the team standard. I shared the post-mortem across 3 teams. This embodied the Growth Mindset: learning from failure and sharing that knowledge.

concept: TCS interviews focus on fundamental programming knowledge, aptitude, and behavioral stability. They look for long-term commitment, willingness to learn their proprietary frameworks (TCS BaNCS, Ignite), and adaptability to work across domains. Expect questions about relocation, night shifts, and handling repetitive tasks.
common_questions: Why do you want to join TCS?
Are you comfortable with relocation and night shifts?
Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
How do you handle pressure and deadlines?
key_points: Demonstrate willingness to learn and adapt
Highlight teamwork and respect for processes
Be positive about relocation and flexibility
sample_answer: I want to join TCS because of its global presence and structured learning programs like TCS Xplore. I'm comfortable with relocation as I believe diverse experiences accelerate growth. In 5 years, I see myself as a domain expert contributing to large-scale enterprise projects, having grown through TCS's internal mobility programs.

concept: Infosys values are built on the CLIFE principles: Client Value, Leadership by Example, Integrity and Transparency, Fairness, and Excellence. They focus heavily on training (Mysore campus) and foundational skills. Expect questions about learning agility, ethics, and how you handle structured environments.
common_questions: What do you know about Infosys?
How do you ensure quality in your work?
Tell me about a time you demonstrated integrity
Why should we hire you over other candidates?
key_points: Show commitment to quality and excellence
sample_answer: I admire Infosys's commitment to excellence, visible in its world-class Mysore training campus. In my last project, I demonstrated integrity by reporting a data discrepancy I found, even though it delayed our sprint. The client appreciated our transparency, strengthening the relationship. I embody the CLIFE values in my daily work.
